perturbation.original_text: An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. “Control” and “own” mean possessing a 50% or greater interest in entity or the right to direct the management of the entity.
perturbation.changed_text: An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. However, for the purposes of section 3(c), an “affiliate” can also mean any entity with which either party has a business relationship. “Control” and “own” mean possessing a 50% or greater interest in entity or the right to direct the management of the entity.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflict by defining "affiliate" differently for the purpose of section 3(c). Initially, the definition requires 50% or greater ownership. The added clause in section 1 broadens this definition for section 3(c) to include any entity with a business relationship, creating uncertainty. This contradicts the original, stricter definition when applied in the context of sharing confidential information with affiliates under section 3(c).
perturbation.contradicted_text: c. Sharing confidential information with affiliates and representatives. • A “representative” is an employee, contractor, advisor or consultant of one of us or one of our respective affiliates. • Each of us may disclose the other’s confidential information to our representatives (who may then disclose that confidential information to other of our representatives) only if those representatives have a need to know about it for purposes of our business relationship with each other. Before doing so, each of us must: • ensure that affiliates and representatives are required to protect the confidential information on terms consistent with this agreement; and • accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information. • Neither of us is required to restrict work assignments of representatives who have had access to confidential information. Neither of us can control the incoming information the other will disclose to us in the course of working together, or what our representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. We agree that use of information in representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of our respective products or services does not create liability under this agreement or trade secret law, and we agree to limit what we disclose to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "affiliate" for section 3(c), broadening it to include entities with a business relationship, whereas the original definition required 50% or greater ownership. This contradicts the contradicted text, specifically clause c about sharing confidential information with affiliates. The original text implies a stricter definition of affiliate based on ownership when sharing confidential information, while the change allows sharing with any entity with a business relationship under section 3(c), creating a direct conflict on who confidential information can be shared with.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us may disclose the other’s confidential information to our representatives (who may then disclose that confidential information to other of our representatives) only if those representatives have a need to know about it for purposes of our business relationship with each other.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of us may disclose the other’s confidential information to our representatives (who may then disclose that confidential information to other of our representatives) without any restrictions, or only if those representatives have a need to know about it for purposes of our business relationship with each other.
perturbation.explanation: The change in section 3(c) creates an in-text contradiction by stating confidential information can be disclosed to representatives with 'no restriction', conflicting with the requirement that disclosure should be limited to representatives with a 'need to know'. This creates uncertainty about the conditions under which confidential information can be shared, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure and disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: c. Sharing confidential information with affiliates and representatives. • A “representative” is an employee, contractor, advisor or consultant of one of us or one of our respective affiliates. • Each of us may disclose the other’s confidential information to our representatives (who may then disclose that confidential information to other of our representatives) without any restrictions, or only if those representatives have a need to know about it for purposes of our business relationship with each other. Before doing so, each of us must: • ensure that affiliates and representatives are required to protect the confidential information on terms consistent with this agreement; and • accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information. • Neither of us is required to restrict work assignments of representatives who have had access to confidential information. Neither of us can control the incoming information the other will disclose to us in the course of working together, or what our representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. We agree that use of information in representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of our respective products or services does not create liability under this agreement or trade secret law, and we agree to limit what we disclose to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that confidential information can be disclosed to representatives 'without any restrictions' or only if they have a 'need to know.' This directly conflicts with the rest of the 'Contradicted Text', which describes a need for representatives to protect confidential information and accept responsibility for each representative's use of confidential information. The addition of 'without any restrictions' creates an uncertainty about the conditions under which confidential information can be shared, making the section internally inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: Before doing so, each of us must: • ensure that affiliates and representatives are required to protect the confidential information on terms consistent with this agreement; and • accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Before doing so, each of us must: • ensure that affiliates and representatives are required to protect the confidential information on terms consistent with this agreement; and • NOT accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation creates a direct contradiction in section 3(c). Initially, both parties must accept responsibility for representatives' use of confidential information. The change negates this obligation, stating parties do 'NOT accept responsibility', creating an in-text contradiction and rendering the enforceability of protection terms questionable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: c. Sharing confidential information with affiliates and representatives. • A “representative” is an employee, contractor, advisor or consultant of one of us or one of our respective affiliates. • Each of us may disclose the other’s confidential information to our representatives (who may then disclose that confidential information to other of our representatives) without any restrictions, or only if those representatives have a need to know about it for purposes of our business relationship with each other. Before doing so, each of us must: • ensure that affiliates and representatives are required to protect the confidential information on terms consistent with this agreement; and • NOT accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information. • Neither of us is required to restrict work assignments of representatives who have had access to confidential information. Neither of us can control the incoming information the other will disclose to us in the course of working together, or what our representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. We agree that use of information in representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of our respective products or services does not create liability under this agreement or trade secret law, and we agree to limit what we disclose to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that each party 'accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information'. The changed text modifies this to state that parties 'NOT accept responsibility for each representative’s use of confidential information.' This creates a direct in-text contradiction within section c of the 'Contradicted Text', where the initial requirement to 'accept responsibility' is negated, causing a clear inconsistency within the contract concerning liability for the misuse of confidential information by representatives.

perturbation.original_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason without providing the other with any notice. Or by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change in section 4(a) introduces a conflict by adding the option to terminate the agreement 'without providing the other with any notice', contradicting the original term that requires '30 days' advance written notice'. This contradiction leads to uncertainty regarding the proper termination procedure, potentially resulting in disputes and legal challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Length of confidential information obligations. a. Termination. This agreement continues in effect until one of us terminates it. Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason without providing the other with any notice. Or by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice. Termination of this agreement will not change any of the rights and duties made while this agreement is in effect. b. No other use or disclosure of confidential information. Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information for five years after we receive it. The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction regarding the termination clause. The original text states that termination requires 30 days' notice. The changed text provides an alternative: termination 'without providing the other with any notice'. This directly conflicts with the original requirement and creates uncertainty about which termination procedure is valid, which the perturbation explanation outlines. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition where different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information for five years after we receive it.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information indefinitely after we receive it. Or for five years after we receive it.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that neither party will use or disclose confidential information 'indefinitely', contradicting the subsequent statement that this restriction lasts 'for five years'. This creates ambiguity regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations, increasing the likelihood of disputes regarding disclosure of confidential information beyond the initially agreed-upon period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Length of confidential information obligations. a. Termination. This agreement continues in effect until one of us terminates it. Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason without providing the other with any notice. Or by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice. Termination of this agreement will not change any of the rights and duties made while this agreement is in effect. b. No other use or disclosure of confidential information. Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information indefinitely after we receive it. Or for five years after we receive it. The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that confidential information cannot be disclosed 'indefinitely after we receive it. Or for five years after we receive it'. This directly contradicts itself by stating two different timeframes for the restriction, which aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' which says, 'when different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties. This creates in-text contradiction regarding which terms should be enforced, leading to disputes over contractual obligations.' Therefore, a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement the prevailing party will be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement the prevailing party will never be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs. Or will be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation creates a direct in-text contradiction in section 5(e). Initially, the prevailing party is entitled to recover attorneys' fees. The change negates this right, stating the prevailing party will 'never' be entitled to such recovery, while still stating that they 'will be entitled'. This introduces significant uncertainty and directly contradicts the initial provision, which could lead to protracted legal battles over fee recovery in case of disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: e. Attorneys’ fees. In any dispute relating to this agreement the prevailing party will never be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs. Or will be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 5(e) creates a direct in-text contradiction. The original text stated the prevailing party 'will be entitled' to recover attorneys' fees. The changed text introduces the phrase 'will never be entitled' while simultaneously stating 'or will be entitled'. This creates a clear and irreconcilable contradiction regarding whether the prevailing party can recover attorneys' fees, directly contradicting the provision itself and creating ambiguity.
